items.front: What may airport traffic control service be based on?
items.back: Observed or known traffic and airport conditions only.

items.front: What does 3-1-1 exclude as a basis for service?
items.back: Assumptions, guesses, or unknown traffic or airport conditions.

items.front: Who remains responsible for avoiding collision when operating under the CFRs?
items.back: The pilot.

items.front: What can traffic information do for pilots near terminal locations?
items.back: Aid them in avoiding collisions.

items.front: Where does the note say traffic information can be especially useful?
items.back: In Class B, C, or D surface areas, terminal radar service areas, and for transiting aircraft near terminal locations.

items.front: On what basis must airport traffic control service be provided?
items.back: Based ONLY upon observed or known traffic and airport conditions. Not on assumptions. Per 3-1-1.

items.front: When should an aircraft be informed of an observed abnormal condition?
items.back: When requested by the pilot or when the controller deems it necessary.

items.front: What is the basic abnormality-advisory format in 3-1-10?
items.back: "(Item) APPEAR/S (observed condition)."

items.front: What is a representative landing-gear abnormality advisory?
items.back: "Landing gear appears up."

items.front: What is a representative baggage-door abnormality advisory?
items.back: "Rear baggage door appears open."

items.front: What gives the controller authority to initiate an abnormality advisory without being asked?
items.back: The controller's judgment that the observed condition makes the advisory necessary.

items.front: If traffic conditions permit, what requests may be approved in Class C or D surface areas?
items.back: A request to cross the surface area or exceed the surface-area speed limit, subject to the paragraph's speed limits and exception.

items.front: What is the maximum speed that may be approved in the surface area unless the pilot reports a higher required minimum speed?
items.back: 250 knots.

items.front: What kinds of maneuvers should not be approved in Class B, C, or D surface areas unless essential to the flight?
items.back: Unusual maneuvers such as unnecessary low passes, unscheduled flybys, very low practice approaches, or buzz jobs.

items.front: What conditions must exist before the unusual-maneuver exception may be used?
items.back: Only when the activity is under a letter of agreement and will not adversely affect safety or reduce service to other users.

items.front: What note-based situations can justify speeds above 250 knots?
items.back: When the airplane flight manual recommends or requires it, or when normal military operating procedures require it.

items.front: What is the maximum speed in Class C or D surface areas?
items.back: 250 knots (288 mph). Do NOT approve higher unless the pilot informs you a higher minimum speed is required for their aircraft type. Per 3-1-11(a).

items.front: What must local controllers do regarding runway scanning?
items.back: Visually scan runways to the maximum extent possible.

items.front: What is ground control's runway-scanning role?
items.back: Assist local control in visually scanning runways.

items.front: When is ground control's runway-scanning assistance especially important?
items.back: When runways are in close proximity to other movement areas.

items.front: How does 3-1-12 split the runway-scanning work?
items.back: Local scans to the maximum extent possible; ground assists that scan.

items.front: What operational risk drives the extra emphasis in 3-1-12?
items.back: Nearby movement areas can make runway conflicts easier to miss unless both positions help scan.

items.front: What must pilots do before entering Class D airspace?
items.back: Establish two-way radio communications.

items.front: What does a reply of aircraft call sign standby mean for a pilot approaching Class D?
items.back: Two-way communications are established and the pilot may enter the Class D airspace.

items.front: When must a pilot be told to remain outside the Delta airspace and standby?
items.back: When workload or traffic conditions prevent immediate provision of airport traffic control services.

items.front: What phraseology is used when the pilot must stay outside Class D until services can be provided?
items.back: "(A/c call sign) REMAIN OUTSIDE DELTA AIRSPACE AND STANDBY."

items.front: What is the key distinction in 3-1-13 between standby and remain outside Delta airspace and standby?
items.back: Standby establishes communications; remain outside Delta airspace and standby also keeps the aircraft out until services can be provided.

items.front: When are two-way radio communications established for Class D entry?
items.back: When the controller responds with the aircraft's call sign — including just '(call sign) standby.' If services cannot be provided, tell the pilot to remain outside. Per 3-1-13.

items.front: When volcanic ash is present on the airport surface, what taxi practice should be avoided to the extent possible?
items.back: Requiring aircraft to come to a full stop while taxiing.

items.front: What departure practice should be provided to the extent possible when volcanic ash is present on the surface?
items.back: A rolling takeoff.

items.front: Why are full stops discouraged on ash-contaminated surfaces?
items.back: Taxi or takeoff roll on ash-contaminated surfaces can make large amounts of ash airborne again.

items.front: What operational harm can newly airborne volcanic ash cause to following aircraft?
items.back: It can significantly reduce visibility and be ingested by their engines.

items.front: What is the controlling condition for 3-1-14?
items.back: Volcanic ash is present on the airport surface.

items.front: What procedures apply when volcanic ash is present on the airport surface?
items.back:
1. Avoid requiring aircraft to come to a full stop while taxiing.
2. Provide for a rolling takeoff for all departures. Per 3-1-14(a-b).

items.front: What is the basic ATC response to a Three/Four-Hour Tarmac Rule return request?
items.back: Provide the requested return to the ramp, gate, or alternate deplaning area as soon as operationally practical, or advise that it cannot be accommodated because it would create a significant disruption.

items.front: Where are significant-disruption criteria for the Three/Four-Hour Tarmac Rule defined?
items.back: In the facility directive, and they vary by airport.

items.front: What phraseology may be used when sending the aircraft back to a deplaning area?
items.back: "(Identification) TAXI TO (ramp, gate, or alternate deplaning area) VIA (route)."

items.front: What phraseology may be used when the return request cannot be accommodated?
items.back: "(Identification) UNABLE DUE TO OPERATIONAL DISRUPTION."

items.front: What phraseology may be used when delay, rather than outright inability, is the issue?
items.back: "(Identification) EXPECT A (number) MINUTE DELAY DUE TO (ground and/or landing and/or departing) TRAFFIC."

items.front: When may preventive control service be provided?
items.back: Only to aircraft operating in accordance with a letter of agreement.

items.front: When should advice or instructions be issued during preventive control?
items.back: Only if a situation develops that requires corrective action.

items.front: How does preventive control differ from ordinary airport traffic control?
items.back: Repetitious routine approval of pilot action is eliminated.

items.front: When do controllers intervene under preventive control?
items.back: Only when they observe a traffic conflict developing.

items.front: What DoD documents can satisfy the agreement requirement for preventive control?
items.back: Airfield operating instructions, memorandums of understanding, or other DoD-only directives.

items.front: Who has primary responsibility for operations on the active runway?
items.back: The local controller.

items.front: What must ground control do before authorizing a crossing or other use of an active runway?
items.back: Obtain approval from local control.

items.front: What must active-runway coordination include?
items.back: The point or intersection at the runway where the operation will occur.

items.front: What phraseology is used to authorize an active-runway crossing?
items.back: "CROSS (runway) AT (point/intersection)."

items.front: What must the ground controller do when the coordinated runway operation is complete?
items.back: Advise the local controller that the operation is complete.

items.front: How is authorization normally given for taxiing or proceeding on or along an active runway for purposes other than crossing?
items.back: By direct communications on the appropriate local-control frequency.

items.front: What must the local controller do before using a runway not previously designated as active?
items.back: Coordinate with the ground controller.

items.front: What methods may local and ground controllers use to exchange runway and movement-area information?
items.back: Verbal means, flight progress strips, other written information, or automation displays.

items.front: What minimum information must local and ground exchange?
items.back: Aircraft identification and applicable runway, intersection, or taxiway information.

items.front: When must ground notify local about a departing aircraft's runway assignment?
items.back: When the aircraft has been taxied to a runway other than one previously designated as active.

items.front: What special departure position must ground report to local?
items.back: Any aircraft taxied to an intersection for takeoff.

items.front: When must the receiving controller be advised of an aircraft's location before release?
items.back: When the runways in use or the aircraft using them are not visible from the tower or on radar.

items.front: What may an LOA authorize for vehicles, equipment, and personnel in two-way communications with ATC?
items.back: Operation in the runway safety area up to the edge of the runway surface.

items.front: What phraseology may be used for that LOA-covered RSA-edge authorization?
items.back: "PROCEED AS REQUESTED."

items.front: When is "PROCEED AS REQUESTED" not approved phraseology?
items.back: For instructing aircraft, vehicles, equipment, or personnel to cross or operate on a runway.

items.front: What must be true before a departure starts takeoff or an arrival crosses the runway threshold?
items.back: The runway to be used must be free of all known ground vehicles, equipment, and personnel.

items.front: What is the key limit of the LOA authority in 3-1-5?
items.back: It covers the runway safety area up to the runway edge, not unrestricted occupancy of the runway itself.

items.front: Who is responsible for properly positioning the aircraft, vehicle, or equipment at the hold line, sign, or designated point?
items.back: The operator.

items.front: How should vehicles, equipment, or personnel on or near the movement area be described?
items.back: In a manner that assists pilots in recognizing them.

items.front: How should relative position be described in traffic information?
items.back: In an easy-to-understand way, such as to your right or ahead of you.

items.front: What kind of traffic references does 3-1-6 favor?
items.back: Plain descriptions pilots can recognize quickly.

items.front: When using a CTRD, what traffic-advisory phraseology may be used?
items.back: The standard radar traffic-advisory phraseology.

items.front: What is a representative vehicle advisory from 3-1-6?
items.back: "Mower left of Runway Two Seven."

items.front: Before issuing taxi instructions, takeoff clearance, or movement-area authorization, what must be determined?
items.back: The position of the aircraft, personnel, or equipment involved.

items.front: What are the preferred ways to determine position when possible?
items.back: Visually or through use of a display system.

items.front: What is the fallback when ATC cannot determine position visually or by display?
items.back: Use a position report.

items.front: What kinds of authorizations are covered by 3-1-7?
items.back: Taxi instructions, takeoff clearance, and authorization for personnel or equipment to proceed onto the movement area.

items.front: What is the core sequence in 3-1-7?
items.back: Determine position first, then issue the movement-related authorization.

items.front: When must low level wind shear or microburst alerts be issued?
items.back: When reported by pilots, ITWS, or detected on approved wind shear detection systems.

items.front: Who receives low level wind shear or microburst alerts?
items.back: All arriving and departing aircraft.

items.front: When do direct wind shear or microburst alerts stop once ATIS is available?
items.back: After the alert is on the ATIS and pilots indicate they have received the appropriate ATIS code.

items.front: How long must the ATIS carry a wind shear or microburst statement after the last report or indication?
items.back: 20 minutes.

items.front: What is the no-ATIS rule for wind shear or microburst information?
items.back: Broadcast it to all arriving and departing aircraft for 20 minutes after the last report or indication.

items.front: What wind information is issued when an LLWAS alert is received?
items.back: The airport wind and the displayed field boundary wind.

items.front: What phraseology is used for a general low level wind shear advisory?
items.back: "LOW LEVEL WIND SHEAR ADVISORIES IN EFFECT."

items.front: What phraseology is used for a runway-specific ribbon-display alert?
items.back: "(Runway) (arrival/departure) WIND SHEAR/MICROBURST ALERT, (windspeed) KNOT GAIN/LOSS, (location)."

items.front: How long must wind shear alerts be included on ATIS?
items.back: For 20 minutes following the last report or indication of wind shear/microburst. Continue alerting until broadcast on ATIS and pilots acknowledge. Per 3-1-8(a).

items.front: What may an uncertified tower display workstation be used for?
items.back: As an aid to visually locate aircraft or determine their spatial relationship to known geographic points.

items.front: What may not be provided using an uncertified tower display workstation?
items.back: Radar services or radar traffic advisories.

items.front: What may a certified tower radar display be used to determine?
items.back: An aircraft's identification, exact location, or spatial relationship to other aircraft.

items.front: What advisory navigation help may local controllers provide to VFR aircraft with a certified display?
items.back: A direction-bound advisory or a suggested heading.

items.front: Why should pilots not be advised they are in radar contact when local control uses tower radar displays as an aid?
items.back: Because that display use is not treated as radar service and could mislead pilots into assuming radar-service benefits are being provided.

items.front: What must be enabled on the tower display workstation if ASR supporting ASDE is out and MLAT is unavailable?
items.back: The ADS-B indicator.

items.front: What services can an uncertified tower display provide?
items.back: It is an AID only — for visually locating aircraft or determining spatial relationships. NO radar services or traffic advisories. General information like 'to your right' is permitted. Per 3-1-9(a).

items.front: What does have numbers allow you to omit from landing information?
items.back: Runway, wind, and altimeter.

items.front: What does have numbers not prove?
items.back: Receipt of the ATIS broadcast.

items.front: When may ATIS landing information be omitted?
items.back: When the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.

items.front: What kind of fixes should be used for additional position reports?
items.back: Prominent geographical fixes that are easily recognized from the air, preferably depicted on sectional charts.

items.front: What weather information must be issued if either value is below basic VFR minima?
items.back: Ceiling and visibility.

items.front: What wind-related hazard advisory should be issued when available on arrival?
items.back: Low level wind shear or microburst advisories.

items.front: What must the controller ensure if the runway has been shortened and the pilot does not indicate the ATIS code?
items.back: The pilot receives the runway number with a shortened announcement.

items.front: What phraseology may be used for a pattern-entry instruction in landing information?
items.back: Enter left base.

items.front: What is the minimum altitude restriction that may be authorized for a low approach under this paragraph?
items.back: No less than 500 feet above the airport.

items.front: Over which aircraft may a restricted altitude low approach be authorized?
items.back: Over preceding landing or taxiing aircraft, but not over an aircraft holding in position or a departing aircraft.

items.front: When may a restricted altitude low approach be cleared over personnel?
items.back: Only when airport authorities have advised the personnel that the approaches will be conducted.

items.front: What must be told to the approaching aircraft when authorizing a restricted low approach?
items.back: The location of applicable ground traffic, personnel, or equipment.

items.front: What does the note say about using higher altitudes than 500 feet?
items.back: The 500-foot restriction is a minimum, and higher altitudes should be used when warranted.

items.front: What altitude example is given for super or heavy aircraft over unprotected personnel or small aircraft near the runway?
items.back: 1,000 feet is more appropriate.

items.front: What phraseology is used for an altitude-restricted low approach?
items.back: Cleared low approach at or above (altitude). Traffic (description and location).

items.front: What is the minimum altitude for a restricted low approach?
items.back: No less than 500 feet above the airport. Do NOT clear over personnel unless airport authorities have advised them. Per 3-10-10.

items.front: What determines whether a pilot request to remain in closed traffic should be approved?
items.back: Local traffic conditions.

items.front: What phraseology approves closed traffic?
items.back: Left or right closed traffic approved.

items.front: What phraseology denies closed traffic?
items.back: Unable closed traffic, followed by additional information as required.

items.front: What additional instruction may be included with closed-traffic approval?
items.back: A required report position.

items.front: What note applies to segregated helicopter or powered-lift patterns?
items.back: They may be established by letter of agreement or other local operating procedures.

items.front: What is the core controller judgment in closed-traffic requests?
items.back: Approve or disapprove the request based on local traffic conditions.

items.front: At what point in the overhead maneuver is the IFR flight plan canceled?
items.back: At the initial point on the initial approach portion of the maneuver.

items.front: What report request is specifically issued for an overhead maneuver?
items.back: Report initial.

items.front: When may pattern altitude or traffic direction be omitted for an overhead maneuver?
items.back: When they are standard or when the pilot is known to be familiar with the nonstandard procedure.

items.front: When is the break point specified to the aircraft?
items.back: Only if the break point is nonstandard.

items.front: What still must be prevented during overhead maneuver operations?
items.back: Conflicts with departing or missed-approach aircraft.

items.front: What document types should hold overhead coordination requirements when frequent use warrants it?
items.back: A letter of agreement, facility directive, base flying manual, or similar local document.

items.front: At an airport without a functioning control tower, when must the pilot cancel IFR for an overhead maneuver?
items.back: Before executing the overhead maneuver or after landing.

items.front: What must be in effect before military SFO, ELP, or practice precautionary approaches may be authorized?
items.back: A letter of agreement or local operating procedure between the military flying organization and the affected ATC facility.

items.front: What altitude information must be obtained before approving the approach?
items.back: The high-key altitude or applicable practice precautionary maneuvering altitudes.

items.front: What traffic-information duty applies within or adjacent to the flameout maneuvering area?
items.back: Provide traffic information to the SFO or ELP aircraft and other concerned aircraft.

items.front: What report request applies at the entry point for overhead SFO or ELP approaches?
items.back: Report high key or report low key, as appropriate.

items.front: What report request applies at low key?
items.back: Report low key.

items.front: What report request applies to straight-in simulated flameout approaches?
items.back: Report (distance) mile simulated flameout final.

items.front: When are low-approach clearance or alternate instructions normally issued on a straight-in simulated flameout approach?
items.back: At the appropriate position on final, normally no closer than 3 miles.

items.front: What arrival-clearance item must be forwarded when a nonapproach control facility clears an aircraft for a visual approach?
items.back: That the aircraft was cleared for a visual approach.

items.front: What timing item may need to be forwarded to the IFR-jurisdiction facility?
items.back: Aircraft arrival time.

items.front: What status items may need to be forwarded to the IFR-jurisdiction facility?
items.back: Cancellation of IFR flight plan and information on missed, unreported, or overdue aircraft.

items.front: When may forwarding items be eliminated?
items.back: When local conditions or situations are fully covered in a letter of agreement or facility directive.

items.front: When must current weather be issued to aircraft executing an instrument approach under this paragraph?
items.back: When weather is below 1,000 feet or 3 miles, or the highest circling minimums if greater, and it changes from the ATIS or previously forwarded weather.

items.front: What runway-related item is on the forwarding list to the IFR-jurisdiction facility?
items.back: Runway in use.

items.front: What is the core same-runway arrival separation rule?
items.back: Do not let the arrival cross the landing threshold until the preceding aircraft is clear of the runway, unless a listed distance exception applies.

items.front: Between sunrise and sunset, what threshold distance lets a Category I arrival land behind a landed Category I or II aircraft when landmarks are available?
items.back: 3,000 feet.

items.front: Between sunrise and sunset, what threshold distance lets a Category II arrival land behind a landed Category I or II aircraft when landmarks are available?
items.back: 4,500 feet.

items.front: What threshold distance applies if the preceding departure is airborne and either aircraft is Category III?
items.back: 6,000 feet.

items.front: Which succeeding aircraft may use visual separation instead of same-runway distance minima?
items.back: A helicopter or powered-lift aircraft.

items.front: What wake-advisory information must be issued in the listed same-runway or close-parallel cases?
items.back: Issue a wake turbulence advisory and include the traffic position, altitude if known, and direction of flight.

items.front: Which following aircraft is specifically included in the same-runway wake-advisory list?
items.back: A Category I aircraft landing behind a Category E aircraft.

items.front: How do the same-runway arrival-separation figures use threshold distance?
items.back: During daylight with suitable landmarks, a landed aircraft need not be clear if the applicable distance from the landing threshold exists before the next arrival crosses the threshold.

items.front: What must be issued to each aircraft operating on intersecting runways during arrival operations?
items.back: Traffic information.

items.front: When may an arrival continue behind a departure on an intersecting runway or intersecting flight path?
items.back: When the departure has passed the intersection or flight path, or is airborne and turning to avert any conflict.

items.front: What preceding-arrival condition can protect an arrival on an intersecting runway or intersecting flight path?
items.back: The preceding arrival is clear, completed landing roll and will hold short, or has passed the intersection or flight path.

items.front: What must be done to both aircraft in the LAHSO case under this paragraph?
items.back: Issue traffic information to both aircraft and obtain an acknowledgment from each.

items.front: No later than when must the LAHSO conditions in subparagraphs b1 and b2 be met?
items.back: No later than landing clearance, and early enough for the pilots to choose other action if desired.

items.front: If no operational benefit is lost during simultaneous LAHSO landings, which aircraft should be restricted?
items.back: The aircraft of the lesser weight category, if known.

items.front: What interval applies to Category B through I arrivals behind a departing Category A aircraft on a crossing runway without radar separation?
items.back: 3 minutes.

items.front: What wake-advisory information must be issued for the listed crossing-runway arrival cases?
items.back: Issue a wake caution and include the traffic position, altitude if known, and direction of flight.

items.front: What must be stated first in a landing clearance?
items.back: The runway number.

items.front: What must be requested if hold-short instructions are not read back with the landing clearance?
items.back: Read back hold short instructions.

items.front: What phrase must preface a landing clearance when the landing runway is changed?
items.back: Change to runway (number), then restate the runway number with the landing clearance.

items.front: What should you do if an aircraft appears aligned with the wrong surface?
items.back: Inform the pilot and issue control instructions or clearances, including a go-around if needed.

items.front: Without a safety logic system, when may an arrival be cleared on the same runway with traffic holding in position or taxiing to LUAW?
items.back: Only after the aircraft in position exits the runway or starts takeoff roll.

items.front: In the full core alert runway safety logic configuration, when is the same-runway LUAW landing-clearance exception not available?
items.back: When reported weather is less than ceiling 800 feet or visibility less than 2 miles.

items.front: Who must be informed when traffic is authorized to LUAW on the same runway?
items.back: The closest aircraft requesting a full-stop, touch-and-go, stop-and-go, low approach, or the option.

items.front: What word must be included in a landing clearance when runway length has been shortened?
items.back: Shortened.

items.front: When may landing separation be anticipated for a succeeding aircraft in sequence?
items.back: When observed aircraft positions show prescribed runway separation will exist when the aircraft crosses the landing threshold.

items.front: When must traffic information be issued under anticipated landing separation?
items.back: If a preceding arrival has not been previously reported and when traffic will depart prior to the arrival.

items.front: What phrase may be used when another aircraft will depart before the arrival reaches the runway?
items.back: Traffic will depart prior to your arrival.

items.front: What note applies to landing sequence numbers when approach control has already established the runway arrival sequence?
items.back: The landing sequence number is optional.

items.front: How does LUAW affect anticipated landing separation?
items.back: Do not use anticipated separation during LUAW operations except in the specifically authorized safety-logic case.

items.front: What must still be issued when using the authorized LUAW exception to anticipated landing separation?
items.back: Applicable traffic information.

items.front: What must the controller determine before issuing a landing clearance under anticipated separation?
items.back: That the prescribed runway separation will exist when the aircraft crosses the landing threshold.

items.front: What must be included in the landing clearance if the aircraft should be seen but has not been visually observed?
items.back: Not in sight and the landing runway restated.

items.front: What phrase begins the landing clearance in the not-visually-observed case?
items.back: Not in sight.

items.front: What must be restated in the not-visually-observed landing clearance?
items.back: The landing runway.

items.front: What satisfies the visually observed requirement in this paragraph?
items.back: Observation of the aircraft on the CTRD.

items.front: What situation requires the not in sight landing-clearance phraseology?
items.back: When the aircraft reports at a position where it should be seen but has not been visually observed.

items.front: What should not be done even if an approach appears to involve a regulatory violation?
items.back: Do not withhold a landing clearance indefinitely.

items.front: What possible explanation does the paragraph give for an apparent violation on approach?
items.back: It might be the result of an emergency situation.

items.front: What should ATC do in any event when an apparent violation is observed on approach?
items.back: Assist the pilot to the extent possible.

items.front: What enforcement-style response does this paragraph reject?
items.back: Using indefinite withholding of landing clearance as the response to the apparent violation.

items.front: Why should landing clearance not be withheld indefinitely in this situation?
items.back: Because the apparent violation may actually be caused by an emergency.

items.front: May a landing clearance be withheld indefinitely for an apparent violation?
items.back: NO. The apparent violation might be the result of an emergency. In any event, assist the pilot to the extent possible. Per 3-10-8.

items.front: When must local control provide taxi instructions after landing?
items.back: When compliance is required before changing to ground control, or when the aircraft must enter an active runway to taxi clear of the landing runway.

items.front: When should runway-exiting or taxi instructions not normally be issued to a landing aircraft?
items.back: Prior to, or immediately after, touchdown.

items.front: Without specific ATC instructions, what is a landing aircraft expected to do?
items.back: Taxi clear of the landing runway, but not onto an intersecting runway unless authorized by ATC.

items.front: What must the controller do if runway hold short instructions are not read back?
items.back: Request the readback.

items.front: What may trigger a read back hold instructions request even away from a runway hold point?
items.back: The controller may initiate it for any point on a movement area when the readback is believed necessary.

items.front: What must local and ground control protect if an aircraft must enter it to clear the landing runway?
items.back: The taxiway, runway, or ramp intersection involved.

items.front: What phraseology can be used to issue a runway-exit instruction with a hold-short requirement?
items.back: Turn left at Charlie, hold short of Runway Two Seven Right.

items.front: What phraseology is used when a wheeled helicopter or powered-lift aircraft taxis on the surface?
items.back: Use normal taxi and ground movement phraseology.

items.front: What defines hover-taxi?
items.back: Slow movement above the surface, normally below 20 knots and in ground effect.

items.front: Why might hover-taxi or air-taxi be necessary instead of ground taxi?
items.back: Rough, soft, or uneven terrain may require it for safety.

items.front: What rotor-system hazard may cause a helicopter to lift off suddenly to avoid damage?
items.back: Ground resonance.

items.front: What defines air-taxi?
items.back: Expeditious point-to-point movement, normally below 100 feet AGL and above 20 knots.

items.front: What is the preferred method for helicopter movements on airports when conditions permit?
items.back: Air-taxi.

items.front: Unless otherwise requested or instructed, what altitude is expected during air-taxi?
items.back: Below 100 feet AGL.

items.front: Who selects a safe airspeed during air-taxi?
items.back: The pilot.

items.front: What kind of clearance should be avoided near taxiing or hover-taxi helicopters?
items.back: A clearance requiring small aircraft or helicopters to taxi in close proximity to them.

items.front: Whenever possible, what should be issued instead of extended hover-taxi or air-taxi operations?
items.back: A takeoff clearance.

items.front: What turn-and-route phrase begins the normal helicopter or powered-lift takeoff clearance?
items.back: Make right or left turn for the specified departure or departure route.

items.front: What protective instruction may be added instead of an avoid instruction in helicopter takeoff clearance?
items.back: Remain in a specified direction of runways, terminals, parking areas, or similar areas.

items.front: When is own-risk departure phraseology used?
items.back: For a reasonable departure from a non-movement area, an unauthorized area, or off the airport.

items.front: What words deliver the own-risk helicopter departure instruction?
items.back: Departure from (location) will be at your own risk.

items.front: What is the downwind takeoff limit without pilot agreement?
items.back: Do not issue it if the tailwind exceeds 5 knots.

items.front: What constitutes agreement to the downwind departure?
items.back: The pilot requests takeoff from a given point in a given direction.

items.front: Which words end the normal helicopter or powered-lift takeoff clearance?
items.back: Cleared for takeoff.

items.front: When may a departing helicopter or powered-lift aircraft take off behind a preceding departure?
items.back: When the preceding departure has left the takeoff area.

items.front: When may a departing helicopter or powered-lift aircraft take off behind a preceding arrival?
items.back: When the preceding arrival has taxied off the landing area.

items.front: How are air-taxiing helicopters or powered-lift aircraft within the airport boundary treated for this rule?
items.back: They are considered taxiing aircraft.

items.front: What traffic pairing does paragraph 3-11-3 address?
items.back: A departing helicopter or powered-lift aircraft in relation to other helicopters or powered-lift aircraft.

items.front: Is touchdown or a stop on the landing area enough to release the next helicopter departure?
items.back: No. The arrival must taxi off the landing area.

items.front: Is becoming airborne enough to release the next helicopter departure behind a preceding departure?
items.back: No. The preceding departure must leave the takeoff area.

items.front: When may an arriving helicopter or powered-lift aircraft land behind a preceding arrival?
items.back: When the preceding arrival has come to a stop or taxied off the landing area.

items.front: When may an arriving helicopter or powered-lift aircraft land behind a preceding departure?
items.back: When the preceding departure has left the landing area.

items.front: Is touchdown alone enough to release a following helicopter arrival behind a preceding arrival?
items.back: No. The preceding arrival must come to a stop or taxi off the landing area.

items.front: Is becoming airborne alone enough to release a following helicopter arrival behind a preceding departure?
items.back: No. The preceding departure must leave the landing area.

items.front: What type of operation does paragraph 3-11-4 govern?
items.back: Arrival separation of a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft from other helicopters or powered-lift aircraft.

items.front: What minimum distance is required between helicopter or powered-lift landing or takeoff points for simultaneous operations?
items.back: At least 200 feet.

items.front: What second condition must be met before simultaneous helicopter or powered-lift operations may be authorized?
items.back: The courses to be flown must not conflict.

items.front: What may be used to determine the 200-foot minimum?
items.back: Surface markings.

items.front: What alternate spacing instruction may be issued if needed?
items.back: Instruct a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft to remain at least 200 feet from another helicopter or powered-lift aircraft.

items.front: Is 200 feet by itself enough to authorize simultaneous operations?
items.back: No. The courses to be flown also must not conflict.

items.front: What is required for simultaneous helicopter landings or takeoffs?
items.back: At least 200 feet between landing/takeoff points AND courses must not conflict. Refer to surface markings or instruct helicopter to maintain 200 feet. Per 3-11-5.

items.front: Whenever possible, what should be issued instead of extended hover-taxi or air-taxi operations on arrival?
items.back: A landing clearance.

items.front: What approach phrase begins the normal helicopter or powered-lift landing clearance?
items.back: Make approach straight-in, circling left, or circling right to the specified location or route.

items.front: What protective instruction may be included in a normal helicopter landing clearance?
items.back: Hold short of a runway or extended centerline, or remain a specified direction or distance from another area or aircraft.

items.front: When is own-risk landing phraseology used?
items.back: For a reasonable landing to a non-movement area, an unauthorized area, or off the airport.

items.front: What words deliver the own-risk helicopter landing instruction?
items.back: Landing at (location) will be at your own risk.

items.front: What is the downwind landing limit without pilot agreement?
items.back: Do not issue it if the tailwind exceeds 5 knots.

items.front: What constitutes agreement to the downwind landing?
items.back: The pilot requests landing at a given point from a given direction.

items.front: Which words end the normal helicopter or powered-lift landing clearance?
items.back: Cleared to land.

items.front: When do the sea-lane provisions in this section apply?
items.back: Where sea lanes are established and controlled.

items.front: Does section 3-12 apply to any water area used occasionally by floatplanes?
items.back: No. It applies only to established and controlled sea lanes.

items.front: What is the key prerequisite before sea-lane procedures in this section are used?
items.back: The sea lane must be established and controlled.

items.front: What kind of operation belongs under section 3-12?
items.back: An established, controlled sea-lane operation.

items.front: What normally releases a departure behind a preceding departure on the same sea lane?
items.back: The preceding departure crosses the end of the sea lane or turns to avert conflict.

items.front: What normally releases a departure behind a preceding arrival on the same sea lane?
items.back: The preceding landing aircraft taxis out of the sea lane.

items.front: If landmarks let you determine distances, what minimum applies when only Category I aircraft are involved in same-sea-lane departures?
items.back: 1,500 feet.

items.front: If landmarks let you determine distances, what minimum applies when a Category I aircraft departs behind a Category II aircraft?
items.back: 3,000 feet.

items.front: If landmarks let you determine distances, what minimum applies when either aircraft is Category III on departure?
items.back: 6,000 feet.

items.front: What note applies when instructing a floatplane to line up and wait?
items.back: Use caution because the aircraft will continue to move, and follow line up and wait with takeoff or other clearance as soon as practical.

items.front: What are the sea lane departure separation distances?
items.back:
Category I behind Category I: 1,500 feet.
Category I behind Category II: 3,000 feet. Per 3-12-2(a)(1-2).

items.front: What normally releases an arrival behind a preceding arrival on the same sea lane?
items.back: The preceding arrival lands and taxis out of the sea lane.

items.front: What normally releases an arrival behind a preceding departure on the same sea lane?
items.back: The preceding departure crosses the end of the sea lane or turns to avert conflict.

items.front: Between sunrise and sunset, what threshold distance allows a Category I arrival behind a Category I or II arrival that has landed but remains in the sea lane?
items.back: 2,000 feet.

items.front: Between sunrise and sunset, what threshold distance allows a Category II arrival behind a Category I or II arrival that has landed but remains in the sea lane?
items.back: 2,500 feet.

items.front: If either aircraft is Category III behind an airborne departure, what threshold distance applies?
items.back: 6,000 feet.

items.front: What conditions are required for the daylight landmark exception behind a preceding arrival?
items.back: It must be between sunrise and sunset, landmarks must allow distance judgment, the other aircraft must have landed, and the correct threshold distance must exist.

items.front: When are ATC light signals used?
items.back: When radio communications cannot be employed.

items.front: Who may be controlled by ATC light signals under 3-2-1?
items.back: Aircraft and the movement of vehicles, equipment, and personnel on the movement area.

items.front: What source gives the meanings of the ATC light signals used under 3-2-1?
items.back: Table 3-2-1.

items.front: What does steady green mean to an aircraft on the ground under Table 3-2-1?
items.back: Cleared for takeoff.

items.front: What does flashing green mean to an aircraft on the ground under Table 3-2-1?
items.back: Cleared to taxi.

items.front: What does the alternating red-and-green warning signal mean?
items.back: General warning signal: exercise extreme caution.

items.front: When should the general warning signal be directed because of traffic conflict?
items.back: When aircraft are converging and a collision hazard exists.

items.front: When should the general warning signal be directed because of aircraft condition?
items.back: When mechanical trouble exists of which the pilot might not be aware.

items.front: What other hazards can justify the general warning signal?
items.back: Other hazardous conditions requiring intensified alertness, such as obstructions, a soft field, or ice on the runway.

items.front: Is the general warning signal prohibitive?
items.back: No. It is not prohibitive and may be followed by another light signal as circumstances permit.

items.front: How does a daylight fixed-wing aircraft on the ground acknowledge a light signal when it has receiver only?
items.back: Move ailerons or rudder.

items.front: How does a daylight fixed-wing aircraft in flight acknowledge a light signal when it has receiver only?
items.back: Rock the wings.

items.front: How does a fixed-wing aircraft acknowledge a light signal between sunset and sunrise when it has receiver only?
items.back: Flash navigation or landing lights.

items.front: How does a hovering helicopter acknowledge a light signal during daylight when it has receiver only?
items.back: Turn toward the controlling facility and flash the landing light, or rock the tip path plane.

items.front: How does a helicopter in flight acknowledge a light signal during daylight when it has receiver only?
items.back: Flash the landing light or rock the tip path plane.

items.front: How does a helicopter acknowledge a light signal between sunset and sunrise when it has receiver only?
items.back: Flash the landing light or search light.

items.front: What does flashing white mean to vehicles, equipment, and personnel under Table 3-2-1?
items.back: Return to starting point on the airport.

items.front: Who is responsible for observing and reporting landing-area conditions?
items.back: Airport management or the military operations office.

items.front: What must be done with a report that affects the safe use of a landing area?
items.back: Relay it to airport management or military operations, copy it verbatim, and record the submitter's name.

items.front: How are landing-area condition reports from unauthorized airport or FAA sources handled?
items.back: Confirm them unless that function belongs to the military operations office.

items.front: What is required if airport management or the operator cannot be contacted about an unsafe condition?
items.back: Issue a NOTAM publicizing the unsafe condition and inform management or the operator as soon as practicable.

items.front: Who can legally close a runway?
items.back: Only airport management or the military operations office.

items.front: How is a RwyCC issued on ATIS?
items.back: State the runway number, the three runway-segment condition codes, and the time of the report.

items.front: What is done with an updated RwyCC before ATIS can be changed?
items.back: Issue it verbally to aircraft until the ATIS broadcast is updated.

items.front: What is issued if no RwyCC is available?
items.back: Only factual runway-surface information, such as reported precipitation accumulation, from the airport operator or pilots.

items.front: What is the first response to a pilot requesting use of a closed or unsafe runway?
items.back: Inform the pilot that the runway is closed or unsafe.

items.front: What must be done if the pilot persists in asking to use a closed or unsafe runway?
items.back: Quote the applicable NOTAM and inform the pilot that a clearance cannot be issued.

items.front: If the pilot still insists and the operation will not adversely affect other traffic, what is the final controller response?
items.back: State that the operation will be at the pilot's own risk.

items.front: Does the controller issue a takeoff or landing clearance for a closed or unsafe runway after the pilot accepts the risk?
items.back: No. The controller states that the operation is at the pilot's own risk but does not issue the clearance.

items.front: When may the ILS associated with a closed runway still be used for approaches?
items.back: Only when the side-step exception applies or when not using the ILS would adversely affect airport efficiency.

items.front: What risk test applies before telling a pilot a closed-runway operation will be at the pilot's own risk?
items.back: In the controller's opinion, the operation must not adversely affect other traffic.

items.front: What procedure when a pilot insists on using a closed/unsafe runway?
items.back:
1. Inform pilot runway is closed/unsafe.
2. If pilot persists, quote the NOTAM and state clearance cannot be issued.
3. If pilot still insists and it won't adversely affect traffic, inform operation is at own risk. Per 3-3-2.

items.front: When must airport condition information be issued?
items.back: In time for it to be useful to the pilot for safe operation.

items.front: What construction-related condition belongs in a timely airport advisory?
items.back: Construction work on or immediately adjacent to the movement area.

items.front: What surface-quality condition belongs in a timely airport advisory?
items.back: Rough portions of the movement area.

items.front: What winter-contamination condition belongs in a timely airport advisory?
items.back: Braking conditions caused by ice, snow, slush, or water.

items.front: What snow-related details should be issued when pertinent?
items.back: Snowdrifts or piles of snow on or along the edges of the area and the extent of any plowed area.

items.front: What traffic-related surface condition belongs in a timely airport advisory?
items.back: Parked aircraft on the movement area.

items.front: What lighting condition belongs in a timely airport advisory?
items.back: Irregular operation of part or all of the airport lighting system.

items.front: How should volcanic ash be described in an airport-condition advisory when known?
items.back: State that volcanic ash is on the airport surface and whether the ash is wet or dry.

items.front: What approved terms may be used for braking action?
items.back: Good, good to medium, medium, medium to poor, poor, and nil.

items.front: What should be done if a pilot reports braking action in nonapproved terms?
items.back: Ask the pilot to categorize the braking action using the approved terms.

items.front: What does the term nil mean in braking-action reporting?
items.back: Bad or no braking action.

items.front: What aircraft detail must be included with a braking-action report?
items.back: The type of aircraft from which the report was received.

items.front: How should braking action be described if only part of the runway is affected?
items.back: Use wording pilots can understand easily, normally runway portions such as first half, last half, or beyond an intersection.

items.front: Why should runway portions be used instead of off-runway landmarks in braking-action reports?
items.back: Extraneous landmarks may be difficult for a landing pilot to identify, especially at night or in low visibility.

items.front: Who must receive runway surface condition and or RCR information if it is provided?
items.back: All USAF and ANG aircraft.

items.front: When is the RCR issued to aircraft other than USAF or ANG?
items.back: Upon pilot request.

items.front: What are the approved braking action terms?
items.back: Good, good to medium, medium, medium to poor, poor, or nil. If a pilot uses non-approved terms, ask them to recategorize. Per 3-3-4(a).

items.front: When are braking action advisories in effect, and what follows?
items.back: When reports are medium, poor, or nil, or conditions are rapidly changing. Put the advisory on ATIS, issue the latest report to arrivals and departures, advise the airport operator, and solicit PIREPs.

items.front: What is the normal operating rule for arresting systems, and when are they raised?
items.back: They normally remain down. Raise them on pilot or military request, or when a military jet with trouble may need engagement, then advise the pilot of the indicated barrier or cable position.

items.front: What does an FFM remote status alarm require during a CAT II or III approach?
items.back: If the aircraft has not passed the inner marker or CAT II missed approach point, issue a caution that the runway localizer is unreliable.

items.front: What triggers the emergency-lighting rule?
items.back: Awareness that an emergency has occurred or will occur.

items.front: What must the controller do under the emergency-lighting rule?
items.back: Take action to provide for operation of all appropriate airport lighting aids as required.

items.front: Must a pilot formally declare the emergency before 3-4-1 applies?
items.back: No. Controller awareness that the emergency has or will occur is enough.

items.front: Does 3-4-1 apply only at night?
items.back: No. The trigger is the emergency, not the time of day.

items.front: At night, when are runway edge lights turned on for departures?
items.back: Before an aircraft taxies onto the runway and until it leaves the Class B, C, or D surface area.

items.front: At night, when are runway edge lights turned on for IFR arrivals?
items.back: Before the aircraft begins final approach.

items.front: At night, when are runway edge lights turned on for VFR arrivals?
items.back: Before the aircraft enters the Class B, C, or D surface area.

items.front: When are runway edge lights turned off for arrivals?
items.back: After the aircraft has taxied off the landing runway.

items.front: What daytime visibility trigger applies runway edge lights?
items.back: Surface visibility less than 2 miles.

items.front: What rule applies to runway edge lights when a closure NOTAM is in effect?
items.back: Do not turn them on for takeoff, landing, or approach use on the closed runway.

items.front: Does the closure-NOTAM rule preclude lighting unaffected runway portions for taxiing, surface vehicles, maintenance, or repair?
items.back: No.

items.front: How are HIRL, runway centerline lights, and touchdown zone lights normally operated?
items.back: By Table 3-4-8 unless facility directives, pilot request, or controller judgment call for a different setting.

items.front: What daytime HIRL/RCLS/TDZL setting applies when visibility is less than 1 mile and or the appropriate RVR equivalent applies?
items.back: Step 5.

items.front: What daytime HIRL/RCLS/TDZL setting applies when visibility is 1 to but not including 2 miles and or the appropriate RVR equivalent applies?
items.back: Step 4.

items.front: What daytime HIRL/RCLS/TDZL setting applies when visibility is 2 to but not including 3 miles?
items.back: Step 3.

items.front: What nighttime HIRL/RCLS/TDZL setting applies when visibility is less than 1 mile and or the appropriate RVR equivalent applies?
items.back: Step 4.

items.front: What nighttime HIRL/RCLS/TDZL setting applies when visibility is 1 to but not including 3 miles and or the appropriate RVR equivalent applies?
items.back: Step 3.

items.front: What nighttime HIRL/RCLS/TDZL setting applies when visibility is 3 to 5 miles inclusive?
items.back: Step 2.

items.front: What nighttime HIRL/RCLS/TDZL setting applies when visibility is more than 5 miles?
items.back: Step 1.

items.front: How are HIRL that control the associated MALSR normally operated?
items.back: By the HIRL-associated-with-MALSR table unless pilot request or controller judgment calls for a different setting.

items.front: What daytime HIRL-with-MALSR setting applies when visibility is less than 1 mile?
items.back: Step 5.

items.front: What daytime HIRL-with-MALSR setting applies when visibility is 1 to but not including 2 miles?
items.back: Step 4.

items.front: What daytime HIRL-with-MALSR setting applies when visibility is 2 to but not including 3 miles?
items.back: Step 3.

items.front: What nighttime HIRL-with-MALSR setting applies when visibility is less than 1 mile?
items.back: Step 4.

items.front: What nighttime HIRL-with-MALSR setting applies when visibility is 1 to but not including 3 miles?
items.back: Step 3.

items.front: What nighttime HIRL-with-MALSR setting applies when visibility is 3 to 5 miles inclusive?
items.back: Step 2.

items.front: What nighttime HIRL-with-MALSR setting applies when visibility is more than 3 miles?
items.back: Step 1.

items.front: What step-down technique ensures the MALSR ends at the intended lower brightness?
items.back: Rotate the brightness control below the intended step and then back to the appropriate step.

items.front: Who must be informed of HIRL changes that affect RVR?
items.back: The appropriate approach controller or PAR controller.

items.front: When should HIRL changes affecting RVR be coordinated if possible?
items.back: In advance.

items.front: What type of HIRL change requires coordination under 3-4-13?
items.back: A change that affects RVR.

items.front: Does 3-4-13 call for coordination only after pilots report the light change?
items.back: No. The coordination is in advance if possible.

items.front: Is HIRL/RVR coordination aimed at tower-only positions?
items.back: No. It is aimed at the appropriate approach or PAR controller.

items.front: How are MIRL normally operated?
items.back: By Table 3-4-10 unless pilot request or controller judgment changes the setting.

items.front: What MIRL step applies during the day when visibility is less than 2 miles?
items.back: Step 3.

items.front: What MIRL step applies during the day when visibility is 2 to 3 miles?
items.back: Step 2.

items.front: What MIRL step applies at night when visibility is less than 1 mile?
items.back: Step 3.

items.front: What MIRL step applies at night when visibility is 1 to 3 miles?
items.back: Step 2.

items.front: What MIRL step applies at night when visibility is more than 3 miles?
items.back: Step 1.

items.front: What exceptions can change the normal MIRL setting?
items.back: Pilot request or controller judgment, if not contrary to the pilot's request.

items.front: When are high speed turnoff lights operated?
items.back: Whenever the associated runway lights are used for arriving aircraft.

items.front: How long do high speed turnoff lights remain on for an arrival?
items.back: Until the aircraft has entered a taxiway or passed the last light.

items.front: What local exception can change the normal high speed turnoff light rule?
items.back: A facility directive to meet local conditions.

items.front: What pilot-based exception can change the normal high speed turnoff light rule?
items.back: Pilot request.

items.front: Is touchdown itself the normal endpoint for high speed turnoff lights?
items.back: No.

items.front: What is one normal endpoint besides entering a taxiway for arrival-use high speed turnoff lights?
items.back: Passing the last light.

items.front: How are taxiway lights normally operated?
items.back: By the applicable taxiway-light table unless facility directives, pilot request, or controller judgment changes the setting.

items.front: For a three-step taxiway-light system, what daytime setting applies when visibility is less than 1 mile?
items.back: Step 3.

items.front: For a three-step taxiway-light system, what night setting applies when visibility is less than 1 mile?
items.back: Step 2.

items.front: For a three-step taxiway-light system, what night setting applies when visibility is 1 mile or more?
items.back: Step 1.

items.front: For a five-step taxiway-light system, what daytime setting applies when visibility is less than 1 mile?
items.back: Step 5.

items.front: For a five-step taxiway-light system, what night setting applies when visibility is less than 1 mile?
items.back: Step 4.

items.front: For a five-step taxiway-light system, what night setting applies when visibility is 1 mile or more?
items.back: Step 3.

items.front: For a one-step taxiway-light system, when is the system used during the day?
items.back: When visibility is less than 1 mile.

items.front: For a one-step taxiway-light system, what is the normal night status?
items.back: On.

items.front: When are obstruction lights turned on if controls are provided?
items.back: Between sunset and sunrise.

items.front: What condition must be true before the obstruction-light timing rule applies?
items.back: Controls are provided.

items.front: Does 3-4-17 tie obstruction-light use to visibility below VFR minima?
items.back: No.

items.front: Does 3-4-17 require a pilot request before obstruction lights are turned on?
items.back: No.

items.front: When is the rotating beacon turned on at night if controls are provided?
items.back: Between sunset and sunrise.

items.front: What daytime weather trigger turns the rotating beacon on?
items.back: Reported ceiling or visibility below basic VFR minima.

items.front: Does 3-4-18 require an IFR flight plan before the beacon is used by day?
items.back: No.

items.front: Is the rotating beacon only a night aid under 3-4-18?
items.back: No.

items.front: What reported weather elements can trigger daytime beacon use?
items.back: Ceiling or visibility.

items.front: How is the runway status light system normally operated?
items.back: Continuously, with automatic intensity settings, except under the listed conditions.

items.front: What must ATC do if a pilot or vehicle reports RWSL illuminated, the entire runway is visible, the runway is clear, and the lights remain on?
items.back: Turn the lights off and reissue the clearance.

items.front: What must ATC use if part of the runway is not visible from the tower during an RWSL report?
items.back: The ASDE system.

items.front: How is the RWSL system treated when the status displays Lost Comm with System?
items.back: As out of service until technical operations personnel check and confirm it operational.

items.front: Once RWSL is turned off, when may it be returned to service?
items.back: Only by technical operations personnel.

items.front: What may ATC do with RWSL intensity upon pilot request?
items.back: Adjust the light intensity.

items.front: Does continuous RWSL operation mean ATC may ignore a report that lights remain on for a clear runway?
items.back: No.

items.front: What must be true before ATC turns RWSL off and reissues the clearance after a report of lights still illuminated?
items.back: The runway must be observed clear, by direct view or by ASDE if part of the runway is not visible from the tower.

items.front: When are REIL operated if separate on-off controls are provided?
items.back: When the associated runway lights are lighted, subject to local directives and pilot request.

items.front: What is one normal turn-off trigger for REIL after an arrival?
items.back: After the arriving aircraft has landed.

items.front: What is one normal turn-off trigger for REIL after a departure?
items.back: After the departing aircraft has left the traffic pattern area.

items.front: What discretionary turn-off trigger exists for REIL?
items.back: When it is determined that the lights are of no further use to the pilot.

items.front: What daytime REIL setting applies when visibility is 2 to 5 miles inclusive on a three-step system?
items.back: Setting 2.

items.front: How is REIL intensity normally selected?
items.back: Use the values in the REIL table unless facility directives or pilot request call for different operation.

items.front: When must a remotely switched VASI be operated?
items.back: When it serves the runway in use, using the applicable intensity table unless an exception applies.

items.front: What is the two-step VASI day setting?
items.back: High.

items.front: What is the two-step VASI night setting?
items.back: Low.

items.front: What is the three-step VASI twilight setting?
items.back: Medium.

items.front: What is the three-step VASI night setting?
items.back: Low.

items.front: How does a basic FAA-standard VASI system operate?
items.back: By photoelectric device, with no on-off control feature, and intended for continuous operation.

items.front: What exceptions can change the normal VASI intensity setting?
items.back: Facility directives to meet local conditions and pilot requirement.

items.front: When must a remotely switched PAPI be operated?
items.back: When it serves the runway in use, using the applicable intensity table unless an exception applies.

items.front: What is the normal day setting for a five-step PAPI?
items.back: Step 4.

items.front: What is the normal night setting for a five-step PAPI?
items.back: Step 3.

items.front: Which PAPI steps are pilot-request settings?
items.back: Steps 1, 2, and 5.

items.front: How does a basic FAA-standard PAPI system operate?
items.back: By photoelectric device, with no on-off control feature, and intended for continuous operation.

items.front: What exceptions can change the normal PAPI intensity setting?
items.back: Local facility directives and pilot request.

items.front: When are approach lights operated between sunset and sunrise?
items.back: When they serve the landing runway or serve a runway to which an approach is being made while aircraft will land on another runway.

items.front: What daytime weather trigger requires approach lights?
items.back: Ceiling less than 1,000 feet or prevailing visibility 5 miles or less while approaches are being made in one of the listed runway or airport situations.

items.front: What is one daytime approach-light case involving landing on another runway?
items.back: Approaches are being made to a runway served by the lights, but aircraft will land on another runway.

items.front: What is one daytime approach-light case involving approaches to the airport rather than a specific runway?
items.back: Approaches are being made to the airport and landing will be made on a runway served by the lights.

items.front: What nonstandard triggers can still justify approach-light operation?
items.back: Pilot request or controller judgment, if not contrary to the pilot's request.

items.front: What does the note say about approach lights when they are not needed?
items.back: Turn the ALS off when not needed for aircraft operations in the interest of energy conservation.

items.front: Does the daytime weather trigger require both low ceiling and low visibility together?
items.back: No. Either ceiling less than 1,000 feet or visibility 5 miles or less is sufficient.

items.front: How are ALS intensity settings normally chosen?
items.back: By the ALS visibility table, unless facility directives, pilot request, or controller judgment calls for a different setting.

items.front: What daytime ALS step applies when visibility is less than 1 mile?
items.back: Step 5.

items.front: What daytime ALS step applies when visibility is 1 to but not including 3 miles?
items.back: Step 4.

items.front: What night ALS step applies when visibility is less than 1 mile without a pilot request for brighter settings?
items.back: Step 3.

items.front: What night ALS step applies when visibility is 1 to 3 miles inclusive?
items.back: Step 2.

items.front: When may ALS step 4 or 5 be used at night?
items.back: Only when requested by a pilot.

items.front: What local factors may justify a facility-directive exception to the ALS table?
items.back: Local atmospheric, topographic, and twilight conditions.

items.front: What system relationship limits SFL operation?
items.back: SFL is a component of the ALS and cannot be operated when the ALS is off.

items.front: What is the main visibility trigger for SFL operation?
items.back: Visibility less than 3 miles while instrument approaches are being made to the runway served by the associated ALS.

items.front: What pilot-driven exception can trigger SFL operation?
items.back: Pilot request.

items.front: What controller-driven exception can trigger SFL operation?
items.back: Controller judgment when deemed necessary, if not contrary to pilot request.

items.front: Can SFL operate independently if the ALS is off?
items.back: No.

items.front: How are MALSR or ODALS with separate on-off and intensity controls normally operated?
items.back: By the applicable visibility table, unless facility directives, pilot request, or controller judgment call for a different setting.

items.front: For a three-step MALSR or ODALS system, what daytime setting applies when visibility is 2 to 5 miles inclusive?
items.back: Setting 2.

items.front: For a three-step MALSR or ODALS system, what nighttime setting applies when visibility is less than 1 mile?
items.back: Setting 3.

items.front: At a part-time tower with a two-step MALSR or ODALS and no duplicate FSS controls, what setting is required during darkness when the tower is unstaffed?
items.back: Low intensity.

items.front: At a part-time tower with a three-step MALSR or ODALS and no duplicate FSS controls, what must be activated during darkness when the tower is unstaffed?
items.back: The air-to-ground radio link.

items.front: If a three-step MALSR or ODALS has no radio air-to-ground control during darkness while the tower is unstaffed, what setting is required?
items.back: Intensity setting 2.

items.front: What exceptions can change the normal MALSR or ODALS table setting?
items.back: Facility directives, pilot request, or controller judgment if not contrary to pilot request.

items.front: When is ALSF-2 operated under 3-4-9?
items.back: When prevailing visibility is 3/4 mile or less or RVR is 4,000 feet or less.

items.front: Does ALSF-2 require both low visibility and low RVR together?
items.back: No. Either prevailing visibility 3/4 mile or less or RVR 4,000 feet or less is enough.

items.front: What system is operated when the ALSF-2 trigger is no longer a factor?
items.back: SSALR.

items.front: What two exceptions can affect ALSF-2 operation while the low-visibility trigger is active?
items.back: Pilot request and controller judgment if not contrary to pilot request.

items.front: If visibility improves above 3/4 mile and RVR rises above 4,000 feet, what happens to the ALSF-2/SSALR selection?
items.back: Operate SSALR because the ALSF-2 trigger is no longer a factor.

items.front: When must ALSF-2 be operated?
items.back: When prevailing visibility is 3/4 mile or less OR RVR is 4,000 feet or less. Also as requested by pilot or deemed necessary by controller. Per 3-4-9(a).

items.front: Who determines which runway or runways are designated runway in use, active runway, or duty runway?
items.back: The ATCT supervisor or controller in charge.

items.front: What runway is normally assigned when wind is 5 knots or more?
items.back: The runway most nearly aligned with the wind.

items.front: What runway is normally assigned when wind is less than 5 knots?
items.back: The calm-wind runway.

items.front: What two listed exceptions may override the normal wind-based runway selection rule?
items.back: Use of another runway is operationally advantageous, or a Runway Use Program is in effect.

items.front: What takes precedence over delay, capacity, and noise-abatement considerations in runway selection?
items.back: Tailwind and crosswind considerations.

items.front: How may ATC handle a pilot request for a different runway?
items.back: ATC may honor it as soon as operationally practicable.

items.front: What must ATC tell a pilot if the requested runway is noise-sensitive?
items.back: Advise the pilot that the requested runway is noise-sensitive.

items.front: How is the active runway determined?
items.back: The ATCT supervisor/CIC determines it. Assign runway most aligned with wind when 5 knots or more. Use calm wind runway when less than 5 knots. Tailwind/crosswind take precedence over delay and noise. Per 3-5-1(a-c).

items.front: When may a designated STOL runway be assigned?
items.back: Only when requested by the pilot or as specified in a letter of agreement with an aircraft operator.

items.front: Is controller convenience by itself enough to assign a designated STOL runway?
items.back: No.

items.front: What must ATC issue if the pilot requests the measured STOL runway length?
items.back: The measured STOL runway length.

items.front: What letter-of-agreement relationship can authorize assignment of a designated STOL runway?
items.back: A letter of agreement with an aircraft operator.

items.front: Does asking for measured STOL runway length automatically authorize assignment of the STOL runway?
items.back: No.

items.front: When a tailwind component exists, what wind information must always be stated?
items.back: Both wind direction and velocity.

items.front: Does ATIS replace the requirement to state wind direction and velocity when a tailwind exists?
items.back: No.

items.front: What note is given about describing the wind in 3-5-3?
items.back: The wind may be described as calm when appropriate.

items.front: If a tailwind component exists, may ATC state only the tailwind component instead of wind direction and velocity?
items.back: No.

items.front: When must ASDE operational status be determined?
items.back: During the relief briefing or as soon as possible after assuming responsibility.

items.front: What is ASDE used to augment?
items.back: Visual observation of landing and departing aircraft and movement-area traffic.

items.front: How must ASDE systems with safety logic be operated?
items.back: Continuously.

items.front: When must an ASDE system without safety logic be operated continuously at night?
items.back: Between sunset and sunrise.

items.front: What visibility trigger requires continuous operation of an ASDE system without safety logic?
items.back: Visibility less than the most distant point in the active movement area.

items.front: What controller-judgment trigger requires continuous operation of an ASDE system without safety logic?
items.back: When the controller judges that its use will assist performance of duties.

items.front: After MLAT or ADS-B loss, what remains in ASDE-X or ASSC radar-only mode?
items.back: Full core safety alert status.

items.front: After MLAT or ADS-B loss, what does ASDE-X or ASSC radar-only mode not provide?
items.back: Automatic data block capability.

items.front: How must ASDE systems be operated?
items.back:
ASDE with safety logic: CONTINUOUSLY.
ASDE without safety logic: continuously sunset to sunrise, and when visibility is less than the most distant point on the movement area. Per 3-6-1(b)(1-2).

items.front: What are the three approved ways to identify an observed ASDE target or track?
items.back: Correlate it with a pilot or vehicle operator report, controller visual observation, or an identified ASR or CTRD target.

items.front: How may an observed ASDE target or track be identified as false?
items.back: By visual observation.

items.front: If a suspected false-target area is not visible from the tower, who may be used for visual observation?
items.back: Airport operations vehicles or pilots in the area.

items.front: When may a false ASDE track be temporarily dropped?
items.back: After positive verification by report or controller visual observation that the track is false.

items.front: What happens to a track when it is temporarily dropped?
items.back: It is removed from the display and from safety logic.

items.front: What form must be annotated when a false track is temporarily dropped?
items.back: FAA Form 7230-4.

items.front: What may ASDE-derived information be used to formulate on the movement area?
items.back: Clearances and control instructions to aircraft and vehicles.

items.front: What may ASDE-derived information be used to determine on the movement area?
items.back: The exact location or spatial relationship of aircraft and vehicles.

items.front: What compliance function may ASDE-derived information support?
items.back: Monitoring compliance with control instructions on taxiways and runways.

items.front: What pilot report function may ASDE-derived information support?
items.back: Confirming pilot-reported positions.

items.front: What taxi-guidance function may ASDE-derived information support?
items.back: Providing directional taxi information, as appropriate.

items.front: When may specific navigational guidance such as exact headings be provided on the movement area?
items.back: Only in an emergency or by mutual agreement with the pilot.

items.front: Who remains responsible for visual navigation to the clearance limit and for avoiding parked or taxiing aircraft, vehicles, or persons?
items.back: The pilot.

items.front: What runway-use action is prohibited if an unidentified target or track is displayed on the runway?
items.back: Do not allow an aircraft to begin departure roll or cross the landing threshold.

items.front: What is the first response to a safety-logic alert?
items.back: Immediately assess it visually and on the ASDE display.

items.front: What is the required response when an arrival aircraft, still airborne before the threshold, activates a warning alert?
items.back: Issue go-around instructions.

items.front: What is the required response when an arrival aircraft gets a warning alert to a taxiway?
items.back: Issue go-around instructions.

items.front: What must a controller do if two arrivals, or an arrival and a departure, activate an alert?
items.back: Issue go-around instructions or take other appropriate action to ensure intersecting-runway separation.

items.front: What is the listed exception that may allow a threshold warning alert to be disregarded?
items.back: A known formation flight crossing the threshold when all other factors are acceptable.

items.front: How are other safety-logic alerts handled when they do not fall into the specific go-around cases?
items.back: Issue instructions or clearances based on good judgment and evaluation.

items.front: What satisfies the requirement that the aircraft not land on the alerted approach after a warning alert?
items.back: A side-step or circle-to-land to another runway.

items.front: What action for an ASDE safety logic warning alert before the landing threshold?
items.back: IMMEDIATELY assess visually and via ASDE. For arrivals before threshold: issue GO-AROUND instructions. Exception: known formation flights crossing threshold may be disregarded. Per 3-6-4(a).

items.front: How is movement on the movement area approved or disapproved?
items.back: With specific instructions by radio or directional light signals, except where permitted in an LOA.

items.front: What kind of runway-related conditional instruction is prohibited in surface movement?
items.back: One dependent on an arrival aircraft on or approaching the runway or a departure aircraft established on takeoff roll.

items.front: Name one unconditional surface phrase specifically listed as not approved.
items.back: Examples include THE FIELD IS YOURS, CLEARED ON ALL SURFACES, THE AIRPORT IS YOURS, and PROCEED ON ALL RUNWAYS AND TAXIWAYS.

items.front: What word is not used in taxi or surface-movement authorization?
items.back: Cleared.

items.front: What prefixes are used for aircraft surface instructions instead of cleared?
items.back: Taxi, proceed, or hold, as appropriate.

items.front: What prefixes are used for vehicle, equipment, or personnel surface instructions?
items.back: Proceed or hold.

items.front: When is measured distance issued for an intersection departure?
items.back: To any pilot who requests it and to all military aircraft unless covered by directives.

items.front: How is intersection-departure remaining distance rounded?
items.back: Rounded down to the nearest 50 feet.

items.front: Can declared distances be used for ATC purposes?
items.back: No. If pilots ask about them, refer them to the Chart Supplement or AIP.

items.front: When is the term full length not used for departures?
items.back: When the runway length available for departures has been temporarily shortened.

items.front: What must every taxi clearance or ground route include?
items.back: The specific route to follow.

items.front: What must be confirmed when a taxi clearance to a runway is issued?
items.back: That the aircraft has the correct runway assignment.

items.front: How can a pilot's taxi read back confirm runway assignment?
items.back: A read back that includes the runway assignment can be considered confirmation.

items.front: If you intend to hold traffic short of a runway, how should the route be issued?
items.back: Issue the route up to the runway hold short point.

items.front: What does the absence of holding instructions authorize traffic to cross?
items.back: All taxiways that intersect the taxi route.

items.front: What clearance is required for each runway an aircraft's taxi route crosses?
items.back: A runway crossing clearance.

items.front: When may multiple runway crossings be issued with a single clearance?
items.back: Only where runway centerlines are 1,300 feet or less apart and the required approval has been received.

items.front: Do follow instructions replace runway crossing clearances?
items.back: No. A runway crossing clearance must still be issued.

items.front: When must read back hold instructions be requested?
items.back: When the runway hold short read back is not received.

items.front: When are progressive taxi or ground movement instructions issued?
items.back: When requested, when needed due to traffic or field conditions, or during reduced visibility when necessary.

items.front: What is the key lesson from the taxi-route figure in 3-7-2?
