items.back: Taxi and vehicle clearances must include the specific movement-area route; runway crossing and hold-short points must be explicit.

items.front: What taxi-power demand should be avoided for super or heavy aircraft?
items.back: Greater than normal taxiing power.

items.front: What proximity should be avoided for small aircraft or helicopters?
items.back: Close proximity to taxiing or hover-taxi helicopters.

items.front: What general caution applies to smaller aircraft or helicopters near larger aircraft or helicopters?
items.back: Use caution when taxiing them in the vicinity of larger aircraft or helicopters.

items.front: What cautionary advisory may be used for a Boeing 757 blast hazard?
items.back: Jet blast.

items.front: What cautionary advisory may be used for helicopter wash hazards?
items.back: Rotor wash.

items.front: What cautionary advisory may be used for propeller wash hazards?
items.back: Prop wash.

items.front: What are the two listed ways to hold traffic clear of a runway?
items.back: Hold short of a specific runway, or hold at a specified point.

items.front: What additional information is issued when necessary while holding traffic clear of a runway?
items.back: Traffic information.

items.front: For a runway number, what hold-short phrase is used?
items.back: HOLD SHORT OF (runway number).

items.front: Who is responsible for establishing hold lines and signs?
items.back: The airport manager.

items.front: Who is responsible for properly positioning the aircraft, vehicle, or equipment at the hold line or designated point?
items.back: The operator.

items.front: When is access to the ILS critical area controlled for arriving aircraft?
items.back: When the official weather observation is below 800-foot ceiling or 2-mile visibility.

items.front: What arrival position trigger is used for basic ILS critical-area protection?
items.back: The arriving aircraft is inside the ILS outer marker or the fix used in lieu of it.

items.front: What two conditions remove the basic ILS critical-area restriction for an arrival?
items.back: The aircraft has reported the runway in sight, or it is circling to land on another runway.

items.front: Name one listed localizer critical-area exception.
items.back: A preceding arriving aircraft landing or exiting, or a preceding departing aircraft or missed approach, may pass through or over the area.

items.front: What tighter localizer trigger applies below 200-foot ceiling or RVR 2,000 feet?
items.back: Do not authorize operations in or over the localizer critical area when the arrival is inside the middle marker or, without one, 1/2 mile final.

items.front: When is the glideslope critical area protected?
items.back: When the arrival is inside the OM or equivalent fix and weather is below 800-foot ceiling or 2-mile visibility, unless the runway is in sight or the aircraft is circling to another runway.

items.front: What advisory is issued if an autoland or coupled approach is announced and the critical area will not be protected in 800-and-2 or better weather?
items.back: ILS CRITICAL AREA NOT PROTECTED.

items.front: Who may define precision approach critical-area criteria at military controlled airports?
items.back: The Department of Defense.

items.front: When must ILS critical areas be protected?
items.back: When official weather is ceiling less than 800 feet OR visibility less than 2 miles. Do not authorize entry when an arriving aircraft is inside the outer marker unless pilot reports runway in sight. Per 3-7-5(a).

items.front: When must the POFZ be clear of traffic?
items.back: When a vertically guided arrival is within 2 miles of the threshold and weather is below 300-foot ceiling or 3/4-mile visibility.

items.front: When must the final approach OCS be clear of aircraft and vehicles?
items.back: When a vertically guided arrival is within 2 miles of the threshold and weather is below 800-foot ceiling or 2-mile visibility.

items.front: What do the POFZ and close-in final approach OCS protect?
items.back: Aircraft executing a missed approach.

items.front: What kind of surfaces can penetrate the POFZ?
items.back: Only horizontal surfaces, such as wings.

items.front: What vehicles are exempt from the POFZ or OCS restriction?
items.back: Vehicles under 10 feet high, needed for maintenance of the airport or navigation facilities, operating outside the movement area.

items.front: If the POFZ or OCS cannot be cleared before the aircraft reaches 2 miles from the threshold in below-minimum weather, what must be done?
items.back: Issue traffic to the landing aircraft.

items.front: What continuing clearing requirement applies to the POFZ and OCS?
items.back: They must be cleared as soon as practical.

items.front: What missed-approach advisory phraseology is used for traffic right of the runway?
items.back: IN THE EVENT OF MISSED APPROACH TAXIING AIRCRAFT RIGHT OF RUNWAY.

items.front: When must the POFZ be clear?
items.back: When an aircraft on a vertically-guided final approach is within 2 miles of the threshold AND weather indicates ceiling below 300 feet or visibility less than 3/4 SM. Per 3-7-6(a).

items.front: How is the sequence of arriving and departing aircraft established?
items.back: By requiring them to adjust flight or ground operation as necessary to achieve proper spacing.

items.front: What does a cleared-for-the-option clearance permit?
items.back: A touch-and-go, low approach, missed approach, stop-and-go, or full-stop landing.

items.front: Where may the cleared-for-the-option procedure be used?
items.back: Only at locations with an operational control tower, subject to ATC approval.

items.front: What should a pilot do if an option stop-and-go or full-stop will cause runway delay?
items.back: Inform ATC as soon as possible.

items.front: For helicopter spacing, what may be more practical than course changes?
items.back: Speed adjustments.

items.front: When hold instructions are issued in lieu of a takeoff clearance, what read-back rule applies?
items.back: Read back of hold short instructions applies.

items.front: What sequencing phrase starts a trailing arrival behind traffic on short final?
items.back: NUMBER (landing sequence number), FOLLOW (description and location of traffic).

items.front: Until what point is a touch-and-go aircraft treated as arriving?
items.back: Until touchdown.

items.front: Until what point is a stop-and-go aircraft treated as arriving?
items.back: Until it makes a complete stop.

items.front: Until what point is a low-approach aircraft treated as arriving?
items.back: Until it crosses the landing threshold.

items.front: After the applicable transition point in 3-8-2, how is the aircraft treated?
items.back: As a departing aircraft.

items.front: Why does 3-8-2 matter operationally?
items.back: It tells you when a touch-and-go, stop-and-go, or low approach changes from arrival status to departure status for separation purposes.

items.front: How are touch-and-go, stop-and-go, and low approach classified?
items.back:
Touch-and-go: arriving until touchdown.
Stop-and-go: arriving until complete stop.
Low approach: arriving until crossing landing threshold.
Thereafter: departing. Per 3-8-2.

items.front: What weather condition is required for simultaneous same-direction operations unless visual separation is applied?
items.back: VFR conditions.

items.front: What communication requirement applies to simultaneous same-direction operations?
items.back: Two-way radio communication must be maintained with the aircraft involved.

items.front: What traffic-information requirement applies to simultaneous same-direction operations?
items.back: Pertinent traffic information must be issued.

items.front: What minimum runway centerline spacing applies to Category I or Category II aircraft in SSDO?
items.back: 300 feet.

items.front: What minimum spacing applies if either aircraft is Category III in SSDO?
items.back: 500 feet between runway centerlines or 400 feet between adjacent strips or runway and strip.

items.front: What minimum spacing applies if either aircraft is Heavy in SSDO?
items.back: 700 feet between runway centerlines or 600 feet between adjacent strips or runway and strip.

items.front: What other provisions still apply during simultaneous same-direction operations?
items.back: Applicable wake turbulence provisions.

items.front: What weather condition is required for simultaneous opposite-direction operations?
items.back: VFR conditions.

items.front: What communication and traffic-information requirements apply to opposite-direction operations?
items.back: Maintain two-way radio communication and issue pertinent traffic information.

items.front: What traffic phraseology is used for opposite-direction traffic on a parallel runway or strip?
items.back: TRAFFIC (description) ARRIVING/DEPARTING/LOW APPROACH, OPPOSITE DIRECTION ON PARALLEL RUNWAY/LANDING STRIP.

items.front: Between sunrise and sunset, what minimum runway centerline spacing applies to opposite-direction operations?
items.back: 1,400 feet.

items.front: Between sunrise and sunset, what minimum strip or runway-and-strip edge spacing applies to opposite-direction operations?
items.back: 1,400 feet.

items.front: Between sunset and sunrise, what minimum runway centerline spacing applies to opposite-direction operations?
items.back: 2,800 feet.

items.front: Between sunset and sunrise, what does Table 3-8-2 say about strip or runway-and-strip opposite-direction operations?
items.back: Not authorized.

items.front: When may runway, wind, and altimeter be omitted from departure information?
items.back: When the pilot states have the numbers.

items.front: When must official ceiling and visibility be issued to a departing VFR aircraft?
items.back: When the weather is below VFR conditions.

items.front: What must the taxi clearance include in departure information?
items.back: The specific route to follow.

items.front: What density-altitude advisory may be issued when appropriate?
items.back: Check density altitude.

items.front: What phraseology is used when the departure runway is shortened?
items.back: RUNWAY (NUMBER) SHORTENED.

items.front: Where are nonstandard takeoff minima published?
items.back: In the tabular supplement to FAA instrument approach charts and the appropriate FAA Forms 8260.

items.front: What must be stated first in a takeoff clearance?
items.back: The runway number.

items.front: When must the runway intersection be stated in the takeoff clearance?
items.back: When the aircraft is cleared for takeoff from an intersection.

items.front: When is full length included in the takeoff clearance?
items.back: When one or more aircraft are at intersections and the aircraft being cleared is at the full length of the runway.

items.front: What is the general runway-crossing rule before issuing takeoff clearance?
items.back: Ensure all runways along the taxi route that lead to the departure runway are crossed first, except in the close-proximity runway case in 3-9-10e.

items.front: What phraseology is used when the aircraft cannot fully cross one runway before reaching its departure runway?
items.back: Cross runway (number), runway (number) cleared for takeoff.

items.front: What word must be stated after the runway number when a runway has been temporarily or permanently shortened?
items.back: Shortened.

items.front: When should full length not be used?
items.back: Do not use full length when the runway has been temporarily shortened, and on permanently shortened runways not until the Chart Supplement is updated.

items.front: When may turbine-powered aircraft be considered ready for takeoff if they do not say otherwise?
items.back: When they reach the runway.

items.front: When may a takeoff clearance be canceled after takeoff roll begins?
items.back: Only for the purpose of safety.

items.front: What must accompany a canceled takeoff clearance?
items.back: The pilot must be told the reason.

items.front: What is the approved phraseology to cancel a takeoff clearance?
items.back: Cancel takeoff clearance (reason).

items.front: May a takeoff clearance be canceled after roll begins solely to meet traffic-management requirements or EDCT?
items.back: No.

items.front: Before takeoff roll begins, when may a takeoff clearance be canceled?
items.back: Whenever circumstances require it, with the reason given to the pilot.

items.front: What kind of issue justifies canceling a takeoff clearance after takeoff roll begins?
items.back: A safety issue, such as traffic or another immediate hazard.

items.front: What is the core gate-hold advisory when procedures are in effect?
items.back: GATE HOLD PROCEDURES ARE IN EFFECT. ALL AIRCRAFT CONTACT (position) ON (frequency) FOR ENGINE START TIME.

items.front: What start-up phrases may be used during gate-hold procedures?
items.back: START ENGINES, ADVISE WHEN READY TO TAXI, or ADVISE WHEN READY TO TAXI.

items.front: When must a request to hold in a delay absorbing area be approved?
items.back: When space and traffic conditions permit.

items.front: What must be announced when gate-hold procedures are terminated?
items.back: GATE HOLD PROCEDURES NO LONGER IN EFFECT.

items.front: Who must be advised when gate-hold procedures end?
items.back: All aircraft on the GC or FD frequency.

items.front: Before takeoff, what is normally issued to departing IFR, SVFR, VFR radar-service, and TRSA VFR aircraft?
items.back: The appropriate departure control frequency and beacon code.

items.front: When may the departure control frequency be omitted from the instruction?
items.back: When a SID has been or will be assigned and the departure frequency is published on the SID.

items.front: What phraseology tells the aircraft the departure frequency and beacon code?
items.back: DEPARTURE FREQUENCY (frequency), SQUAWK (code).

items.front: What phraseology tells certain departing IFR military aircraft to switch?
items.back: CHANGE TO DEPARTURE.

items.front: About when are civil aircraft, and military transport and cargo types, normally switched to departure control after takeoff?
items.back: When the aircraft is about 1/2 mile beyond the runway end, if further communication is not required.

items.front: Before what altitude are military turboprop or turbojet aircraft, except transport and cargo types, not asked to make frequency or beacon changes?
items.back: Before reaching 2,500 feet above the surface.

items.front: What is the intent of line up and wait?
items.back: To position an aircraft for an imminent departure when takeoff clearance cannot yet be issued because of traffic.

items.front: What traffic-information rule applies to LUAW?
items.back: Issue traffic information unless the runway traffic is another aircraft taking off or landing on that runway and is clearly visible to the holding aircraft.

items.front: What conditional phrases are prohibited with LUAW?
items.back: Phrases like behind landing traffic or after the departing aircraft.

items.front: What is the normal maximum time an aircraft should remain in LUAW without additional instructions?
items.back: 90 seconds.

items.front: When may LUAW be used if the departure point is not visible from the tower?
items.back: Only when the aircraft position can be verified by ASDE or the runway is used for departures only.

items.front: What nighttime condition is required for intersection LUAW?
items.back: The runway must be used as a departure-only runway, among the other listed conditions.

items.front: When a runway is shortened, what word must be included in the LUAW clearance?
items.back: Shortened.

items.front: What is the purpose of Line Up and Wait (LUAW)?
items.back: To position aircraft for an IMMINENT departure when takeoff clearance cannot be issued due to traffic. Issue traffic information to any aircraft so authorized. Per 3-9-4(a).

items.front: When may takeoff separation be anticipated?
items.back: When there is reasonable assurance the required separation will exist when the aircraft starts takeoff roll.

items.front: Does anticipated separation require the full separation to already exist before issuing takeoff clearance?
items.back: No.

items.front: What is the key decision point in anticipated separation?
items.back: Whether the required separation will exist when takeoff roll begins.

items.front: What kind of assurance is required before using anticipated separation?
items.back: Reasonable assurance.

items.front: When may takeoff clearance be issued before separation exists?
items.back: When there is REASONABLE ASSURANCE that prescribed separation will exist when the aircraft starts takeoff roll. Per 3-9-5.

items.front: What is the basic same-runway departure separation rule?
items.back: Do not let the succeeding aircraft begin takeoff roll until the required departure, arrival-clear, or wake interval condition exists.

items.front: If only Category I aircraft are involved and landmark distance can be determined, what airborne distance is required?
items.back: 3,000 feet.

items.front: If either the succeeding aircraft or both aircraft are Category II, what airborne distance is required?
items.back: 4,500 feet.

items.front: If either aircraft is Category III, what airborne distance is required?
items.back: 6,000 feet.

items.front: When may visual separation be applied in lieu of the distance minima in 3-9-6a?
items.back: When the succeeding aircraft is a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft.

items.front: What is Category I for same-runway separation purposes?
items.back: Small single-engine propeller-driven aircraft weighing 12,500 pounds or less, and all helicopters.

items.front: What is Category II for same-runway separation purposes?
items.back: Small twin-engine propeller-driven aircraft weighing 12,500 pounds or less.

items.front: What is Category III for same-runway separation purposes?
items.back: All other aircraft.

items.front: What interval applies to a Category I aircraft behind a Category E aircraft on the same runway or on a parallel runway separated by less than 700 feet?
items.back: 2 minutes.

items.front: May pilot requests to deviate from the intervals in 3-9-6f through k be approved?
items.back: No.

items.front: What phraseology is used when an aircraft must wait for the required wake interval?
items.back: HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.

items.front: What do the same-runway departure-separation figures emphasize?
items.back: A succeeding aircraft cannot begin takeoff roll until the preceding aircraft has crossed the runway end, turned to avert conflict, or is airborne with the required landmark-based distance.

items.front: How long must a Category I aircraft wait when departing from an intersection behind a departing Category F, G, or H aircraft on the same runway?
items.back: At least 3 minutes after the preceding aircraft has taken off.

items.front: How long must a Category I aircraft wait when departing from an intersection behind a departing Category E aircraft in the listed same-runway or parallel-runway cases?
items.back: At least 3 minutes after the Category E aircraft has taken off.

items.front: For intersection departures on the same runway or parallels less than 2,500 feet apart, what interval applies to Categories B through I behind Category A?
items.back: 4 minutes.

items.front: For the same intersection-departure cases, what interval applies to Categories B through I behind Category B or D?
items.back: 3 minutes.

items.front: For the same intersection-departure cases, what interval applies to Categories E through I behind Category C?
items.back: 3 minutes.

items.front: What phraseology is used when an aircraft must wait for an intersection-departure wake interval?
items.back: HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.

items.front: What operations are considered to be departing from an intersection for wake-turbulence purposes?
items.back: Touch-and-go and stop-and-go operations.

items.front: Is the successive touch-and-go or stop-and-go visual-spacing exception authorized with a Category A lead or departure aircraft?
items.back: No.

items.front: What must be issued when the successive touch-and-go or stop-and-go visual-spacing exception is used?
items.back: A wake turbulence cautionary advisory and the position of the larger aircraft.

items.front: What is the intersection departure wake turbulence separation?
items.back: Category I behind departing Category F/G/H: 3 minutes. Category I behind departing Category E: 2 minutes. Per 3-9-7(a)(1-2).

items.front: What must be issued to each aircraft operating on intersecting runways?
items.back: Traffic information.

items.front: When may a departure begin behind a preceding departure on an intersecting runway?
items.back: When the preceding aircraft has passed the intersection or is turning to avert any conflict.

items.front: What arriving-aircraft condition can protect an intersecting-runway departure after landing?
items.back: The arriving aircraft has completed landing roll and either acknowledged hold short, acknowledged exit before the intersection, is observed turning off before the intersection, or has passed the intersection.

items.front: What LAHSO case can satisfy the intersecting-runway departure rule?
items.back: A civilian arriving aircraft has acknowledged a clearance to land and hold short of the intersecting runway or flight path under LAHSO.

items.front: What wake interval applies to a Category I aircraft behind a Category E aircraft on intersecting runways when projected flight paths will cross?
items.back: 2 minutes from when the preceding aircraft began takeoff roll.

items.front: What is the wake interval for Category B through I aircraft behind Category A aircraft on intersecting runways when projected flight paths will cross?
items.back: 3 minutes from when the preceding aircraft began takeoff roll.

items.front: May a pilot request to deviate from the required wake interval be approved on intersecting runways?
items.back: No, not when the preceding aircraft requires wake turbulence separation.

items.front: When may a departure begin on a nonintersecting converging runway if the flight paths intersect?
items.back: Only when one of the listed protections exists, such as the other aircraft crossing the departure runway, turning away, or holding short of the projected intersection after landing.

items.front: What arriving-aircraft condition can protect a departure on a converging runway?
items.back: The arriving aircraft has completed landing roll and will hold short of the projected intersection, has passed the projected intersection, or has crossed over the departure runway.

items.front: What happens when the extended centerline crossover is within 1 NM of either departure end?
items.back: Apply the intersecting-runway provisions of 3-9-8 unless the facility is using aids specified in a facility directive.

items.front: What wake interval applies to Category B through I aircraft behind Category A aircraft on converging runways when projected flight paths will cross?
items.back: 3 minutes from when the preceding aircraft began takeoff roll.

items.front: What wake interval applies to a Category I aircraft behind a Category E aircraft on converging runways when projected flight paths will cross?
items.back: 2 minutes from when the preceding aircraft began takeoff roll.

items.front: May a pilot request to deviate from the required wake interval be approved on converging runways?
items.back: No, not when the preceding aircraft requires wake turbulence separation.
